---
title: "Bullard, Buckley & Kingsolver (2026, *PLoS Biol*) — relationship to our paper"
date: 2026-05-26
author: "Daniel W.A. Noble (clarification dialogue with Claude)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
---

# Question

A new paper — @bullard2026 (*PLoS Biology*, 21 May 2026) — sounds close to what
our paper is doing. Read it carefully, summarise it, and compare/contrast its
claims with our manuscript. Where are we saying the same things? Where do we
differ? Accuracy is key.

# Short answer

Same *problem class* (the field's constant-shape-across-T assumption fails and
matters for fluctuating-T predictions), but **different data type** (individual
continuous failure times vs our aggregated proportion/count data) and
**different model parameterisation** (a frequentist log-logistic AFT with shape
linear in T vs our hierarchical Bayesian 4PL with T-effects on all four 4PL
parameters and estimated asymptotes). Mathematically, their log-logistic
survival model is the **special case of our 4PL with asymptotes forced to 0/1**;
our framework strictly generalises theirs but on a different (more common) data
structure. This is complementary, not duplicative. The bib entry `@bullard2026`
already exists from the Zotero auto-export but the paper is not yet cited
anywhere in `ms.qmd` / `supplement.qmd` — that needs fixing.

# Summary of Bullard et al. 2026

**Question.** When TDT models are extended from constant to fluctuating
temperatures, two assumptions become load-bearing: (a) *additivity* of injury
accumulation across temperatures [@jorgensen_unifying_2021] and (b) the shape of
the cumulative survival curve $S(t)$ does not change with temperature
[@rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. Do these hold, and what happens when they
don't?

**Model.** Log-logistic accelerated failure time (AFT) survival model:
$$
S(t) = (1 + (t/a)^k)^{-1}
$$
with scale $a$ = median $t_f$ and shape $k$ inversely related to the variance
of log failure time: $\operatorname{var}(t_f) = \pi^2/(3k^2)$. Proportional
hazards (PH) explicitly rejected via LR test ($\chi^2 = 5403, p < 2\times 10^{-16}$).
Log-logistic preferred over Weibull, log-normal, Gompertz, exponential via AIC
(9/11 species).

**Constant-T results** (their Fig 2). Refit individual knockdown times for 11
*Drosophila* species at multiple stressful temperatures (~32.5–43 °C; data from
@jorgensen_drosophila_2019).

- Median $\log(t_f)$ declines linearly with $T$ (TDT location holds; residuals
  in their Fig S2).
- Shape $k$ generally *decreases* (so $\operatorname{var}[\log t_f]$ *increases*)
  with $T$ across species (mixed-effects LR test: $\chi^2 = 33.7, p < 0.0001$),
  invalidating the constant-shape assumption of @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020.
  Heterogeneous across species (their Fig S3).

**Fluctuating-T results** (their Fig 4). Predict median $t_f$ and full survival
curves for 26 sets of *D. melanogaster* under fluctuating 34–42 °C
[@jorgensen_unifying_2021]. Three models compared:

1. @jorgensen_unifying_2021 (deterministic, additive injury).
2. @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020 (probabilistic, single average $S(t)$).
3. Their "increasing-variance" log-logistic (parametric AFT, shape linear in T).

Findings: Jørgensen and log-logistic predict **median $t_f$** comparably well;
both substantially better than Rezende. Log-logistic predicts the **survival
curves** better than Rezende (paired *t*-test, $p = 0.0019$; 20/26 wins).
**All three models underestimate median $t_f$ at lower stressful T**, which they
argue (via flat residuals in their Fig S2) indicates non-additivity of HI
accumulation rather than non-linearity in the TDT trend.

**Discussion.** Mechanistic candidates for increasing variance with T: HSP/ROS
kinetics, individual heterogeneity ("frail" vs "robust"), cryptic genetic
variation expressed only under stress, microbial "tailing" of survival curves,
fanning-out of TDT lines if individuals vary in slope not just intercept.
Explicitly out of scope: permissive temperatures, repair, recovery, full field
conditions.

# Side-by-side comparison

| Dimension | @bullard2026 | This paper (Noble, Arnold & Pottier) |
|---|---|---|
| **Headline claim** | Variance in $\log(t_f)$ changes with T; constant-shape assumption fails; allowing shape to vary improves fluctuating-T predictions | The two-stage pipeline mishandles uncertainty, hierarchy, asymmetric shape; a single Bayesian 4PL recovers everything with calibrated uncertainty |
| **Data type** | Individual continuous failure times | Aggregated proportion/count data at (T × duration) cells; continuous proportions (Beta) for PSII |
| **Model class** | Frequentist parametric AFT (log-logistic), `flexsurv` | Hierarchical Bayesian 4PL on counts/proportions, `brms`; packaged as `bayesTLS` |
| **What varies with T** | scale (linear), shape (linear) | All four 4PL parameters can carry T-effects; `temp_effects` configurable |
| **Asymptotes** | Forced to 0 and 1 | Estimated, with disjoint-bounds reparam |
| **Hierarchy / random effects** | None | Random intercepts on `mid` (date / tank / cup / life stage …) |
| **Uncertainty** | Parametric CIs (`flexsurv`) | Full posteriors through $z$, $CT_{max,1h}$, $T_{crit}$, HI, survival curves |
| **Fluctuating-T prediction** | Time-stepped simulation under time-varying $S(t,T)$; **tested against empirical $D$. *melanogaster* fluctuating data** | Jørgensen HI integral with posterior $z$ & $CT_{max,1h}$; optional Sharpe–Schoolfield repair; **validation via planted-dose recovery on simulated traces** |
| **Comparator critiqued** | @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020 + additivity | Classical two-stage GLM → OLS pipeline |
| **Case studies** | 11 *Drosophila* species (constant T) + 1 fluctuating-T set | Shrimp (lethal + sublethal), zebrafish life stages, snowgum leaf PSII |
| **Repair / recovery** | Out of scope | In scope (Sharpe–Schoolfield) |
| **Software** | `flexsurv` | `bayesTLS` (own package) |
| **Venue** | *PLOS Biology* | *Ecology Letters* (Methods) |

# Genuine overlap (where we are saying related things)

1. **Both reject the constant-shape assumption.** Bullard's empirical
   evidence (shape $k$ varies with T in most *Drosophila*, their Fig 2D)
   directly supports our supplement worked example
   §sec-joint-extend ("temperature effects on every shape parameter"), where
   we simulate T-varying $u$ and $k$ and show the 4PL recovers them. Their
   empirical evidence + our simulation evidence + the `fit_4pl` default of
   T-effects on all four parameters together make a strong package.
2. **Both critique @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020's average-$S(t)$
   assumption.** Our manuscript currently cites Rezende positively as
   foundational; Bullard's critique is more pointed. Worth sharpening ours
   without strawmanning [[feedback_equivalence_framing]].
3. **Both highlight that mean/median estimates aren't enough.** Bullard,
   Discussion: *"studies to date have emphasized estimates or predictions of
   mean or median failure times. However, many important biological questions
   require information about cumulative survival curves…"*. We make the same
   case via Bayesian posteriors over derived quantities.

# Where they go further than us (honest)

1. **Empirical evidence across 11 species that the assumption fails.** We
   *show* (via simulation) that *if* shape varies with T, two-stage estimates
   are biased; they *show* (via real *Drosophila* data) that shape *does* vary
   with T in most species. These complement; we should cite the empirical side.
2. **Direct empirical model comparison under fluctuating T** (their Fig 4).
   Our validation under fluctuating T is via planted-dose recovery on simulated
   traces, not against an empirical fluctuating-T benchmark. Real gap.
3. **Residual analysis suggesting non-additivity of HI accumulation across
   the stressful range** (Bullard, Discussion, "Modeling survival in
   fluctuating temperatures"). Our paper uses the Jørgensen HI integral
   as-is with posterior $z$, $CT_{max,1h}$; we don't test additivity.
4. **Mechanistic discussion of *why* variance changes with T** — cryptic
   genetic variation, individual heterogeneity, HSP/ROS, "tailing", TDT-line
   fanning. Our discussion is structural/statistical; theirs is mechanistic.

# Where we go further than them

1. **Asymptote estimation** — critical for sublethal endpoints (leaf PSII,
   fertility) and partial-mortality designs. Their log-logistic forces $\ell=0$,
   $u=1$. The snowgum PSII case study would not be analysable under their
   framework.
2. **Aggregated count/proportion data** — the dominant assay type in
   ectotherm thermal biology (individual continuous times are the exception, not
   the rule).
3. **Hierarchical structure** with random effects on `mid` for
   experimental nesting (date, tank, cup, life stage).
4. **Bayesian uncertainty propagation through all derived quantities**
   ($z$, $CT_{max,1h}$, $T_{crit}$, HI, $\tau_{eff}$, $S(t)$).
5. **Repair via Sharpe–Schoolfield** in `predict_heat_injury(repair = TRUE)`
   (Bullard list it as future work).
6. **Comparison to the field-dominant two-stage pipeline** (GLM Stage-1 →
   OLS Stage-2), not only @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020. The two-stage is
   the *de facto* default; Rezende is one influential probabilistic model.
7. **`bayesTLS` R package** — reusable, tested, documented.
8. **$T_{crit}$ rate-multiplier definition** [@faber2026] integrated as a
   first-class derived quantity [[project_tcrit_definition]].

# A mathematical fact to flag

Their log-logistic $S(t) = (1 + (t/a)^k)^{-1}$ is the special case of our 4PL
with $\ell = 0$, $u = 1$, $\text{mid} = \log_{10}(a)$. Modulo log base — their
$k$ (natural log) and our 4PL $k$ (log$_{10}$ time) are related by
$k_{\text{theirs}} = k_{\text{ours}} \cdot \ln 10$. This is the same "asymptotes
forced to 0/1" reduction we already criticise as the 2PL/two-stage assumption
(ms.qmd line 170: *"simpler nested models (3PL, with one asymptote fixed; 2PL,
with both fixed at 0 and 1) … can be implemented within the same general
framework"*). The honest framing: **our model is a hierarchical Bayesian
generalisation of the log-logistic AFT of @bullard2026, additionally
estimating asymptotes and accommodating aggregated proportion/count designs.**

# One nuance — the *sign* of $k$ with $T$

Bullard's empirical finding (their Fig 2D, Discussion): in *Drosophila*, the
log-logistic shape $k$ generally **decreases** with $T$ (variance of $\log t_f$
**increases**; survival curves get flatter at hotter $T$). Our supplement
§sec-joint-extend simulates $k$ **increasing** with $T$ (`log_k_T = 0.30`, the
curves get *sharper* at hotter $T$). Likewise the `scen5_sharpen` scenario in
`scripts/simulations/run_simulations.R` (k rises with T). Both are valid violations of
constant-shape, and our framework is sign-agnostic — but we are exploring the
**opposite sign** from Bullard's empirical observation. Options:

1. Add a sentence in the worked example acknowledging this and noting
   sign-agnosticism; cite @bullard2026 for the empirical evidence on the other
   sign.
2. Add a companion scenario / cell with $\log k_T < 0$ (matching Bullard's
   direction) to mirror the empirically-observed case in the simulation.

# Concrete recommendations for the manuscript

Where to cite `@bullard2026` (currently not cited anywhere):

- **Intro, ms.qmd line ~166–168** (two-step pipeline limitations) — as direct
  empirical evidence that the constant-shape assumption fails in real data.
- **Supplement §sec-joint-extend** (T-varying shape worked example) — Bullard's
  Fig 2B,D is the strongest empirical demonstration that shape varies with T in
  ectotherms, motivating exactly the scenario we simulate.
- **Discussion / "Limits of the present analysis"** (currently empty
  subheaders, ms.qmd ~614–626): (a) we don't empirically validate against
  fluctuating-T data the way they do (their Fig 4); (b) we assume Jørgensen
  HI-integral additivity whose validity their residuals call into question
  across the full stressful range.
- **Model section** — note our 4PL subsumes their log-logistic as the
  $\ell=0, u=1$ case (cite as positional framing).
- **Simulation results discussion (ms.qmd ~273–277)** — Bullard's empirical
  evidence strengthens the case that the shape-violation scenarios (scen3,
  scen5) are realistic rather than adversarial.

# Verification status

- I have read the full @bullard2026 PDF (PLOS Biology 24(5): e3003623, 21 May
  2026) once carefully. All section/figure/equation references above are from
  that read; quote-check before paraphrasing into the manuscript.
- Confirmed: `bullard2026` exists in `bib/tdt_problems.bib` (full Zotero
  entry); not cited in `ms.qmd`, `supplement.qmd`, or any current `notes/*.qmd`.
- Confirmed: existing manuscript text at ms.qmd:162, 164, 166, 170, 241, 247
  cites @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020, @rezende_landscapes_2014,
  @jorgensen_drosophila_2019, @jorgensen_unifying_2021, @jorgensen_nature_2022,
  @orsted_finding_2022 — Bullard's reference network overlaps strongly.
- Confirmed: supplement §sec-joint-extend (supplement.qmd:914) simulates
  $u$ declining and $k$ rising with $T$; `log_k_T = 0.30` (positive sign).
- The mathematical-equivalence claim (log-logistic = 4PL with $\ell=0, u=1$) is
  derivable from first principles — no citation needed but should be sanity-
  checked when written into the manuscript prose.
- Related memories: [[project_function_architecture]],
  [[project_tcrit_definition]], [[feedback_equivalence_framing]].
